profile.name: Emily
profile.role_in_household: Widowed primary household head; sole decision-maker and caregiver for two young dependents
profile.financial_literacy_level: Moderate: comfortable discussing account types (taxable vs. retirement vs. cash) and basic tradeoffs, but prefers the advisor to translate tax and withdrawal strategy into clear next steps
profile.risk_attitude_behavioral: Moderate risk taker in principle, but behaviorally cautious after being widowed—she wants growth to keep up with long-term needs while also prioritizing a strong cash buffer and avoiding big drawdowns that would feel destabilizing
profile.communication_style: Warm but direct; uses qualifiers like "I think" and "roughly" when recalling details, asks clarifying questions, and appreciates periodic summaries. Will pause to think, may circle back to the same point (especially around retirement timing and kids) to confirm she understood.
emotional_tendencies: grief-adjacent sensitivity when discussing spouse-related topics
anxiety around "running out" or making an irreversible mistake
relief when there is a clear plan and checklist
profile.decision_making_style: Deliberate and checklist-driven; prefers to review options, sleep on major decisions, and then commit once she understands the tradeoffs and the implementation steps
profile.trust_level_toward_advisor: Cautiously trusting: open to guidance and delegation, but wants transparency on fees, taxes, and why each recommendation fits her moderate risk tolerance and near-retirement timeline
internal_biases: loss aversion (feels losses more strongly than gains)
recency bias (recent market headlines can influence her comfort level)
mental accounting (separates "cash safety" from "investments for growth")
hidden_concerns: worries about coordinating finances alone and missing something important
concerned about providing long-term security for her two dependents while transitioning into retirement
uncertainty about how taxes will affect withdrawals and investment decisions
